column4: negates the base "predator draws on oust" consequence; this is Bitter and Sweet Story's own printed override — fails transfer, no other card is affected. Nearest 6.5, which owns oust consequences generally, not this card's specific exception.

teaches two homeless generals at once: counters stacking cumulatively on the same vampire (nearest 1.2, the recurring stacking gap) and torpor-rescue burning all such counters (nearest 5.3). Neither section's scope claims either half.

the stay-or-burn choice is Blade Clot's own counter mechanic — fails transfer; "allies cannot go to torpor" restates a rule any fluent judge already knows — fails non-obviousness. Nearest 5.3 (torpor) and 5.5 (allies).
!TENSION:no-effect-plays a prohibition bars play when there is no equipment to steal — prohibition side of the question (contrast Draba.3's permissive side)
restates this card's own printed target restriction (cannot be played against an ally) — fails transfer, no other card is affected by this reading.

non-interaction between two named cards (Blood Trade / Consanguineous Boon); no principle transfers beyond the pair — fails transfer. Nearest 1.1, and only loosely.

scope limit of a control-taking effect against Imbued incapacitation, a narrow interaction specific to Imbued mechanics — fails transfer beyond it. Nearest 5.7.
pure two-card interaction (Botched Move / Dark Influences); no general principle and nothing in the taxonomy comes close — fails transfer.
position-based crypt targeting plus secret counter-tracking, unique to Brainwash's own mechanic — fails transfer to other cards' targeting. Nearest 5.1.

complex Orun/Laibon lock-and-bleed interaction unique to this card — fails transfer.
this card's own action requires the Baali clan/discipline to take — a printed gate specific to this card, not a general requirements principle.

cumulative/stacking effects: nothing owns "multiple instances of an effect stack." This is the taxonomy's own cited example for the watch-list gap; 1.2 is nearest but does not claim it.
